nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coastal waters (0–16 m depth) over silty sand and seagrass beds; commonly associates with long-spined sea urchins (Diadema setosum) for shelter

diet.notes: Generally advised to offer meaty foods such as frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, marine flesh, and small feeder shrimp. Wild individuals have been documented feeding on small benthic and planktonic crustaceans at night. Some individuals may initially refuse non-live foods, requiring weaning onto frozen or prepared diets.

avoidWith: conspecifics in groups of three or more
boisterous tankmates that may outcompete them for food

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Jon Hanson (London, UK), originally posted to Flickr 2006-08-05. Shows adult specimen clearly; full-resolution file.
Photo by Peter Halasz (Pengo), taken at Budapest Zoo Aquarium 2018-08-30 with Panasonic DMC-FZ5. Aquarium shot; clearly shows species markings.

summary: Kaudern's Cardinal (Pterapogon kauderni) is a small, distinctive silver reef fish from Indonesia's Banggai Islands, marked by bold black stripes and white spots. Generally considered intermediate in care, it is widely advised to keep as a bonded pair in a reef aquarium.
careNotes:
Kaudern's Cardinal (*Pterapogon kauderni*) is endemic to the Banggai Islands of Indonesia — one of the most geographically restricted marine fish in the world — and is classified as Endangered (IUCN EN) largely due to pressure from the aquarium trade. Captive-bred individuals are generally advised when available, as they tend to accept prepared foods more readily and reduce pressure on wild populations.

In the aquarium, this species is best kept as a bonded pair or singly. Keeping three or more individuals in a confined system has been known to result in persistent aggression between them, so groups of three or more are generally best avoided. Captive lifespans of roughly 3–5 years have been reported.

In the wild, this species shelters among the spines of *Diadema* sea urchins (large, long-spined urchins common on Indo-Pacific reefs); providing urchins or dense rockwork with cave-like openings in the aquarium may help reduce stress. Juveniles have also been observed associating with anemones for protection.
breedingNotes: Kaudern's Cardinal is a paternal mouthbrooder: after spawning, the male incubates the eggs and larvae entirely within his mouth until the young are released as fully formed juveniles at approximately 8 mm in length — there is no free-swimming larval stage. The absence of a planktonic larval phase is widely regarded as what makes this species one of the more accessible marine fish to breed in captivity, and breeding in home aquariums has been reported with reasonable frequency. Distinct pairing behaviour precedes spawning. Sexual maturity is reached at approximately 4.1 cm total length, with a generation time of roughly 1.5 years.

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal mouthbrooder. The male incubates eggs and larvae until juveniles of approximately 8 mm are released fully formed, with no free-swimming larval stage. Captive breeding has been reported with reasonable success.
